title: Human-AI Interaction Bias Taxonomy
subtitle: A curated, Wikipedia-backed taxonomy of human judgment distortions in human-AI interaction.
mission: Use Wikipedia as the primary source base for a codex of how people miscalibrate trust, selectively uptake information, get steered by presentation, and over-attribute mind or capability to AI.

curated_table.why_it_fits: The canonical page for over-weighting automated recommendations and discounting contradictory non-automated evidence.
Explains why system labels, confident answers, and expert framing can trigger deference to AI output.
Captures the shortcut where easy-to-process answers feel more credible, a common response to polished AI output.
Explains how one positive surface trait, such as polished language or interface design, can inflate trust in the whole system.
Directly describes performance loss when automation is trusted enough that people stop actively supervising it.
Relevant because sustained monitoring deteriorates when AI handles routine tasks, making misses harder to catch.
Provides the clearest anchor for under-trust and rejection of algorithmic advice after observed error or perceived threat.
Explains resistance when AI recommendations feel controlling, autonomy-limiting, or imposed.
The central concept for interpreting AI output in ways that confirm prior views and discount disconfirming material.
Explains why people can use AI as a justification engine for desired conclusions rather than as a neutral check.
Directly captures the tendency to seek AI outputs that align with existing beliefs and avoid contradictory answers.
Relevant because people can notice agreeable parts of AI output while overlooking the parts that challenge them.
Explains why users may keep a belief even after AI or source material contradicts it.
Fits because AI can make it easier to surface only the snippets or examples that support a chosen conclusion.
Captures closed loops where repeated, like-minded AI-mediated content strengthens prior beliefs.
Explains how personalized ranking and curation narrow the range of views users encounter through AI systems.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: A direct fit for how initial AI suggestions, estimates, or ranges shift subsequent human judgment.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Captures how equivalent AI outputs can lead to different decisions depending on wording and presentation.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Relevant to model defaults, suggested prompts, and preselected actions that users tend to accept.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Fits AI-mediated option sets where adding one comparison shifts preference toward a target choice.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Explains why AI-highlighted examples or repeatedly surfaced cases feel more representative than they are.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Fits interface-driven emphasis, ranking, and highlighting that steer what users notice first.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Useful for subtle cueing from prompts, examples, and system context that shifts later responses and decisions.

curated_table.category: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
curated_table.why_it_fits: Provides the broad design framework for how AI interfaces structure the options users end up choosing among.
The foundational concept for attributing human traits, motives, or feelings to non-human systems.
The most direct page for applying anthropomorphic attribution specifically to AI systems.
Directly explains why conversational fluency can make users over-attribute understanding or empathy to AI.
Captures the HCI finding that people automatically apply social heuristics to computers and agents.
Useful for interpreting AI behavior as if the system has beliefs, goals, or intentions of its own.
Explains the error of projecting one's own interpretation onto the world, including onto model output and behavior.
Fits cases where users think either they or the model understand a topic more deeply than is actually true.
Relevant to overestimating the reliability or competence of AI based on confident presentation and successful past use.
